content.text: What it is

A north star KPI is a single, carefully chosen metric that captures the core long-term value an agentic AI product delivers to its users and the business. It is north star not because it covers everything, but because it is the one metric the entire team can align around: if this metric is improving, the product is on the right track; if it is declining, everything else is secondary. For agentic AI products, the north star KPI is typically a measure of user outcomes (hours saved per week, cases resolved autonomously per month) rather than a measure of AI quality (eval pass rate) or product usage (sessions per day). The tracking plan defines how the north star is measured, how it rolls up from sub-KPIs, the instrumentation required, and the cadence at which it is reviewed.
Why it matters

Without a north star KPI, AI product teams tend to optimize for whichever metric is easiest to measure — often AI output quality — while losing sight of whether the product is actually changing how users work. A north star provides a forcing function: every feature decision, prompt change, and model upgrade should be evaluated against whether it moves the north star metric. Teams that track north star KPIs also have a much easier time communicating success to executive leadership, because the north star translates AI technical improvements into business language that CFOs and CPOs understand without needing to interpret evaluation scores.
Benefits
Focus and alignment: a single north star prevents metric proliferation and aligns engineers, product managers, and business stakeholders around the same success signal.
Investment justification: north star KPIs translate AI improvements into business terms, enabling clear ROI calculations and investment decisions.
Prioritization discipline: when two features compete for sprint capacity, the decision is made by asking which will move the north star more — eliminating unproductive debates.
Strategic continuity: the north star remains stable across model versions, UI changes, and infrastructure updates, providing a consistent long-term measure of product trajectory.
Early warning signal: when the north star starts declining before users churn or revenue drops, it gives the team lead time to diagnose and respond.
Trade-offs and limitations
North star metrics are lagging: they reflect cumulative user outcomes and change slowly, making them unsuitable for week-to-week engineering iteration guidance.
Single-metric risk: optimizing hard for one metric can lead to gaming or negative side effects on uncovered dimensions (Goodhart's law applied to AI products).
Attribution difficulty: north star changes are hard to attribute to specific product decisions when multiple changes occur simultaneously, requiring controlled experiments.
Instrumentation complexity: measuring outcomes like 'hours saved' requires tracking behavior before and after the AI feature is used within the same workflow, which is often technically complex.
How it works

North star KPI selection follows a three-step process. First, identify the core value exchange: what does the user gain by using the AI product? Examples include time saved, decisions improved, errors avoided, and ideas generated. Second, find the single metric that best captures that value exchange in a measurable form. The metric should be: measurable from product telemetry without user surveys, sensitive to product improvements so it changes when the product gets better, and meaningful to the business so executives care when it improves. Third, identify the two or three sub-KPIs that are leading indicators of the north star — metrics that move faster and predict the north star's direction.

The tracking plan operationalizes the north star by specifying the event schema (what user actions are captured and what properties are recorded), the aggregation logic (how raw events roll up to the KPI value), the review cadence (weekly for sub-KPIs, monthly for north star), the alerting threshold (a 10% week-over-week decline triggers an investigation), and the retarget schedule (quarterly review of whether the north star still captures the intended value exchange as the product matures).
Architecture

attrs.alt: North star KPI sitting above three sub-KPI categories (user value, quality, business), all feeding into a tracking plan
attrs.title: North Star KPI Hierarchy and Tracking Plan

content.text: The north star sits at the top of a three-level hierarchy. Sub-KPIs in three categories — user value, AI quality, and business impact — provide leading indicators and diagnostic signals. The tracking plan at the bottom defines how raw events are instrumented, aggregated, and monitored to produce each level of the hierarchy.
When to use it

Define the north star KPI before launch and revisit it only at major product pivots. The north star should be set when the core value hypothesis is clear enough to measure but before the product has enough production data to be distorted by optimizing for it. Once set, the north star should be treated as a semi-permanent artifact — stable enough that quarterly product reviews can compare against the same metric over time, but reviewed annually to ensure it still captures the product's core value as the feature set evolves.
How to implement

Hold a north star selection workshop with product, engineering, and business stakeholders. Collect 5–10 candidate metrics, then evaluate each against the three criteria: measurable from telemetry, sensitive to improvements, and meaningful to the business.
Select the north star by consensus and document the rationale for choosing it over the rejected candidates. This documentation is valuable when the metric is later challenged.

Identify 2–3 sub-KPIs per dimension (user value, AI quality, business impact) that are leading indicators of the north star. Sub-KPIs are the week-to-week metrics that predict the north star's monthly trend.

Write the tracking plan: define the event schema for every user action that contributes to the north star or a sub-KPI, the aggregation SQL or dbt model that computes the KPI value, and the dashboard configuration.
Implement instrumentation and validate it against a holdout set before launch. Instrument telemetry before deploying the AI feature so that you have baseline data from day one.
Schedule a monthly north star review meeting where the trend is presented alongside the sub-KPIs that explain it. Use this meeting to make quarterly target adjustments.
Examples
North star KPI definition and tracking plan in structured YAML:

# product: analyst-ai-assistant
# version: 1.0.0
# definition date: 2025-09-01

north_star:
  name: "AI-Accelerated Reports Per Analyst Per Week"
  definition: >
    The number of completed analytical reports per active analyst per week
    where the analyst used the AI assistant for at least one step in the workflow.
  measurement:
    event: "report.completed"
    filter: "session.ai_used = true AND analyst.is_active = true"
    aggregation: "count(reports) / count(distinct analysts) OVER (7-day rolling window)"
    granularity: "weekly"
  target:
    baseline_pre_ai: 2.1   # reports/analyst/week before AI launch
    year_1_target:   4.5   # +114% improvement target
    year_2_target:   6.0
  alert:
    drop_threshold_pct: 10  # Alert if 7-day value drops > 10% week-over-week
    owner: "product-eng-oncall"
  rationale: >
    This metric captures the core value exchange: analysts get more done.
    It is directly observable from product events (no survey required),
    sensitive to AI quality improvements, and understood by the CFO.

sub_kpis:
  user_value:
    - name: "AI Step Adoption Rate"
      definition: "% of reports where analyst used AI assistant for ≥ 1 step"
      target: 0.70
      cadence: weekly
    - name: "AI Draft Acceptance Rate"
      definition: "% of AI-generated draft sections accepted with <20% edit"
      target: 0.55
      cadence: weekly

  ai_quality:
    - name: "Eval Pass Rate"
      definition: "% of eval suite test cases passing on current prompt version"
      target: 0.92
      cadence: per_pr
    - name: "Hallucination Rate"
      definition: "% of AI outputs containing unverifiable factual claims"
      target_max: 0.03
      cadence: weekly_sample

  business:
    - name: "Reports Per FTE"
      definition: "Total reports completed / active analyst headcount per month"
      target_lift_pct: 100  # double capacity within 12 months
      cadence: monthly
    - name: "Analyst Retention (AI-using cohort)"
      definition: "12-month retention rate of analysts who actively use AI"
      target: 0.88
      cadence: monthly

tracking_plan:
  events:
    - name: "report.completed"
      properties: [report_id, analyst_id, ai_used, ai_steps_count, time_to_complete_min]
    - name: "ai.draft.generated"
      properties: [report_id, section, model, tokens_in, tokens_out, latency_ms]
    - name: "ai.draft.accepted"
      properties: [report_id, section, edit_distance_pct, time_to_accept_sec]
  infrastructure:
    pipeline: "Segment → BigQuery → dbt → Looker"
    refresh_cadence: "daily at 03:00 UTC"
    retention_days: 730
A dbt model that aggregates the north star KPI from raw events:

-- models/metrics/north_star_ai_reports_per_analyst.sql
-- Computes the north star KPI: AI-accelerated reports per analyst per week

WITH
daily_active_analysts AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('week', completed_at) AS week,
    analyst_id,
    COUNT(DISTINCT report_id) AS reports_completed,
    COUNTIF(ai_used = TRUE) AS ai_assisted_reports,
    AVG(time_to_complete_min) AS avg_completion_time_min
  FROM {{ ref('stg_report_completed') }}
  WHERE analyst_id IN (SELECT analyst_id FROM {{ ref('dim_active_analysts') }})
  GROUP BY 1, 2
),

weekly_kpi AS (
  SELECT
    week,
    SUM(ai_assisted_reports) AS total_ai_reports,
    COUNT(DISTINCT analyst_id) AS active_analysts,
    SAFE_DIVIDE(SUM(ai_assisted_reports), COUNT(DISTINCT analyst_id)) AS north_star_value,
    AVG(avg_completion_time_min) AS avg_time_to_complete
  FROM daily_active_analysts
  GROUP BY 1
),

with_yoy AS (
  SELECT
    *,
    LAG(north_star_value, 52) OVER (ORDER BY week) AS value_52w_ago,
    LAG(north_star_value, 1) OVER (ORDER BY week) AS value_prev_week,
    SAFE_DIVIDE(
      north_star_value - LAG(north_star_value, 1) OVER (ORDER BY week),
      LAG(north_star_value, 1) OVER (ORDER BY week)
    ) AS wow_change_pct
  FROM weekly_kpi
)

SELECT
  week,
  north_star_value,
  active_analysts,
  total_ai_reports,
  avg_time_to_complete,
  wow_change_pct,
  value_prev_week,
  value_52w_ago,
  -- Alert flag: 10% week-over-week drop
  CASE WHEN wow_change_pct < -0.10 THEN TRUE ELSE FALSE END AS alert_threshold_breached
FROM with_yoy
ORDER BY week DESC
Real-world example

A supply chain analytics company built an AI assistant for demand planners. After six months of development, the product team was tracking 17 metrics: response latency, thumbs-up rate, session count, DAUs, feature adoption by screen, eval pass rate, API cost per session, and several others. When leadership asked whether the product was working, the PM took 20 minutes to answer and the answer was unclear — because no single metric captured whether demand planners were actually making better decisions.

A north star workshop in week 26 identified the north star as 'forecast accuracy delta for planners using AI vs. not using AI' — a direct measure of whether the AI was helping planners make better decisions. This required new instrumentation: capturing the forecast values planners submitted, linking them to actual demand outcomes 90 days later, and computing accuracy by planner cohort. The instrumentation took three weeks to build, but once live, it became the single clearest signal the team had ever had. The first 90-day data showed a +12% accuracy improvement for AI-using planners. Leadership approved a 40% budget increase immediately. The other 16 metrics were retained as diagnostic signals but stopped appearing in leadership reviews.
Common mistakes
Choosing a metric that is easy to measure rather than one that captures the core value exchange — usage counts (sessions, API calls) are commonly chosen as north stars but do not measure outcomes.
Choosing a metric that the team cannot influence — if the north star depends on external factors (market conditions, customer budget cycles), it is not a useful product metric.
Defining the north star without a baseline measurement, making it impossible to know whether the product is improving against it.
Changing the north star too frequently in response to quarterly business priorities, which destroys the longitudinal comparability that makes it valuable.
Treating the north star as a target to hit rather than a direction to move in — a team optimizing to hit a specific number is more likely to game the metric than to deliver genuine value.
Best practices
Select the north star by asking: 'If this metric doubled, would the business be undeniably successful?' If the answer is not an immediate yes, reconsider the metric.
Instrument the north star before launch so you have baseline data from day one — retroactive baselining is unreliable.
Review north star trends monthly in an executive-visible dashboard; review sub-KPIs weekly in team sprint reviews.
Document the rejected north star candidates and the reasons for rejection — this context prevents the team from cycling back to weaker metrics when the chosen metric becomes inconvenient.
Use A/B experiments to establish causal attribution between product changes and north star improvements — correlational evidence alone is insufficient for investment decisions.
Performance and scalability

The tracking infrastructure for north star KPIs must be reliable and low-latency enough to be trusted. Stale dashboards or unreliable event pipelines erode confidence in the metric and cause teams to revert to ad hoc measurement. Invest in data pipeline reliability (99.9% event delivery SLA), daily freshness (dashboard updated within 4 hours of midnight UTC), and schema governance (no event schema changes without migration plan) as non-negotiable requirements for the north star tracking infrastructure.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
Segment
Event collection and routing
Schema governance; routes to data warehouse
dbt
KPI aggregation models
Version-controlled SQL; lineage from events to north star
Looker / Tableau
Executive and team dashboards
Certified metrics pinned to dbt models for single source of truth
Monte Carlo / Great Expectations
Data quality monitoring
Detects pipeline failures before they corrupt north star values
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Focus
Limitation
North star KPI (this topic)
Single outcome metric everyone aligns to
Lags; needs sub-KPIs for fast iteration
OKRs (Objectives and Key Results)
Goal framework with multiple key results
Key results rotate quarterly; no longitudinal trend
Balanced scorecard
Multiple dimensions tracked simultaneously
No single signal for prioritization clarity
Security and reliability

North star KPI data should be classified as business-sensitive: it reveals user behavior patterns and product performance trends that could be competitively sensitive. Apply the same access controls to the north star dashboard as to other business-sensitive metrics. For AI features handling regulated data (health, financial), the event schema that feeds the north star must comply with data minimization principles — only capture the attributes necessary to compute the metric, not raw input/output text.
Key takeaways
A north star KPI is a single outcome metric that captures the core value exchange between the AI product and its users — it is not an AI quality metric or a usage count.
The tracking plan is as important as the metric definition: without reliable instrumentation and aggregation, the north star is unmeasurable.
Sub-KPIs in three categories (user value, AI quality, business impact) provide the leading indicators that guide week-to-week iteration while the north star provides monthly direction.
Instrument the north star before launch to establish a baseline — retroactive baselining is unreliable and loses the early trend data that is most valuable for demonstrating impact.
Keep the north star stable for at least 12 months; frequent changes destroy longitudinal comparability and signal a lack of strategic conviction about the product's core value.
Use controlled experiments to establish causal attribution between product improvements and north star gains — correlation alone is insufficient for investment decisions.
